From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

2010 action fantasy film directed by Louis Leterrier

This article is about the 2010 remake of the film. For the 1981 film of the same name, see  [Clash of the Titans (1981 film)](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(1981_film) "Clash of the Titans (1981 film)"). ***Clash of the Titans*** is a 2010 [action](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") [fantasy film](/wiki/Fantasy_film "Fantasy film") and [remake](/wiki/Remake "Remake") of the [1981 film of the same name](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(1981_film) "Clash of the Titans (1981 film)") produced by [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer") (the rights to which had been acquired by [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Entertainment "Warner Bros. Entertainment") in 1996 through its purchase of [Turner](/wiki/Turner_Entertainment "Turner Entertainment")). The story is very loosely based on the [Greek myth](/wiki/Greek_myth "Greek myth") of [Perseus](/wiki/Perseus "Perseus").[[2]](#cite_note-BW_2007-04-2)[[3]](#cite_note-BD_2007-10-3)[[4]](#cite_note-DC_2009-10-4) An international co-production between the United States, United Kingdom, and Australia, the film is directed by [Louis Leterrier](/wiki/Louis_Leterrier "Louis Leterrier") from a screenplay by [Travis Beacham](/wiki/Travis_Beacham "Travis Beacham"), [Phil Hay](/wiki/Phil_Hay_(screenwriter) "Phil Hay (screenwriter)"), and [Matt Manfredi](/wiki/Matt_Manfredi "Matt Manfredi"), and stars [Sam Worthington](/wiki/Sam_Worthington "Sam Worthington"), [Gemma Arterton](/wiki/Gemma_Arterton "Gemma Arterton"), [Mads Mikkelsen](/wiki/Mads_Mikkelsen "Mads Mikkelsen"), [Alexa Davalos](/wiki/Alexa_Davalos "Alexa Davalos"), [Ralph Fiennes](/wiki/Ralph_Fiennes "Ralph Fiennes"), and [Liam Neeson](/wiki/Liam_Neeson "Liam Neeson"). The film was originally set for standard release on March 26, 2010,[[3]](#cite_note-BD_2007-10-3)[[4]](#cite_note-DC_2009-10-4) but it was later announced that the film would be converted to [3D](/wiki/3-D_film "3-D film") and was released on April 2, 2010.[[5]](#cite_note-3D-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Poster-6)

*Clash of the Titans* was a commercial success, grossing $493 million worldwide and becoming one of the highest-grossing films of 2010, but received mostly mixed-to-negative reviews from critics, with praise for its epic scale, visual style, and the performances of Neeson, Fiennes, and Arterton, but criticism for Leterrier's direction, Worthington's performance, and the post-production 3D conversion. The film's success led to a sequel, *[Wrath of the Titans](/wiki/Wrath_of_the_Titans "Wrath of the Titans")*, released in March 2012.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

During the [gods'](/wiki/Twelve_Olympians "Twelve Olympians") [battle](/wiki/Titanomachy "Titanomachy") against the [Titans](/wiki/Titan_(mythology) "Titan (mythology)"), [Hades](/wiki/Hades "Hades") created the [Kraken](/wiki/Cetus_(mythology) "Cetus (mythology)"), a fearsome monster that helped win the gods' victory. [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus") and his brothers then divided the world amongst themselves: Zeus took the skies, [Poseidon](/wiki/Poseidon "Poseidon") the seas, and Hades, deceived by Zeus, was left to rule the [Underworld](/wiki/Greek_underworld "Greek underworld").

Zeus later created the mortal [humans](/wiki/Human "Human"), whose worship maintained the gods' immortality. Over time, however (theoretically after the [Trojan War](/wiki/Trojan_War "Trojan War") & [Odysseus'](/wiki/Odysseus "Odysseus") 10-year delayed return home to [Ithaca](/wiki/Ithaca_(island) "Ithaca (island)")), some mortals began to defy their creators. When the human king [Acrisius](/wiki/Acrisius "Acrisius") leads a siege at [Mount Olympus](/wiki/Mount_Olympus "Mount Olympus"), Zeus has sex with his wife [Danae](/wiki/Danae "Danae"), conceiving a [demigod](/wiki/Demigod "Demigod") son, [Perseus](/wiki/Perseus "Perseus"). Upon discovering this, an enraged Acrisius locks the queen and her newborn child in a chest and throws them into the sea. Zeus retaliates by striking the king with a lightning bolt, which severely deforms him. Perseus and the now-dead Danae are found by the fisherman Spyros, who raises the baby with his wife Marmara.

Eighteen years later, Perseus and his family watch as soldiers from the city of [Argos](/wiki/Argos,_Peloponnese "Argos, Peloponnese") destroy the statue of Zeus, declaring war on the gods. The soldiers are slaughtered by the [Furies](/wiki/Erinyes "Erinyes"), who are controlled by Hades. Hades then destroys the family's fishing vessel; Spyros and his family drown, with Perseus as the only survivor.

Found by another group of soldiers, Perseus is brought before King [Kepheus](/wiki/Cepheus,_King_of_Aethiopia "Cepheus, King of Aethiopia") and Queen [Cassiopeia](/wiki/Cassiopeia_(Queen_of_Ethiopia) "Cassiopeia (Queen of Ethiopia)"), who are celebrating their campaign against the gods. While their daughter [Princess Andromeda](/wiki/Andromeda_(mythology) "Andromeda (mythology)") disapproves of her parents' rebellion, Cassiopeia furthers her boasts. The revelry is suddenly interrupted by Hades. He kills Cassiopeia by rapidly aging her, threatens to unleash the Kraken upon Argos in ten days unless Andromeda is offered to it as a sacrifice, and reveals Perseus's godly heritage. Perseus meets Io, a mysterious woman cursed with immortality, who confirms his origin.

Perseus, Io, and the King's Guard led by Draco journey to the [Stygian Witches](/wiki/Graeae "Graeae"), seeking a way to defeat the Kraken. To help his son, Zeus tries to give Perseus a sword forged on Olympus, which he refuses. Soon after, they are attacked by the corrupted Acrisius, now known as Calibos, who is working for Hades. During the fight, Draco severs Calibos' hand, forcing him to retreat to a desert where the blood from his injury conjures giant [scorpions](/wiki/Scorpion "Scorpion") which attack the group. They are rescued by a band of [djinn](/wiki/Jinn "Jinn"), desert sorcerers who tame the remaining scorpions and lend their aid to Perseus and his group.

They arrive at the lair of the Stygian Witches, who are forced to reveal a weapon to defeat the Kraken: the head of the [gorgon](/wiki/Gorgon "Gorgon") [Medusa](/wiki/Medusa "Medusa"), who resides in the Underworld. Upon arrival, Perseus and his remaining companions enter Medusa's temple lair, while Io remains outside. Medusa kills everyone except Perseus, who finally manages to behead her by using the reflective underside of his shield to see her with his back turned. Upon leaving Medusa's lair, Calibos appears and fatally stabs Io. As Perseus and Calibos fight, Perseus picks up the Olympian sword and kills his opponent; Calibos' human form is briefly restored, and he begs Perseus not to become like the gods before he dies. As Io lies dying, she urges Perseus to save Andromeda and Argos.

The winged horse [Pegasus](/wiki/Pegasus "Pegasus") arrives and takes Perseus back to Argos while Hades, having manipulated Zeus and the gods into earning their trust, releases the Kraken. Prokopion, a fanatical worshipper of Hades, and his followers take Andromeda to be sacrificed. Perseus arrives in time and exposes Medusa's head to the Kraken, which gradually petrifies and crumbles. Prokopion attempts to kill Perseus, but Kepheus intervenes, and both of them are then crushed by the Kraken's falling claw. Hades confronts Perseus, but the latter, invoking Zeus, hurls his sword at Hades, forcing him back to the Underworld.

Perseus rescues Andromeda, who asks him to rule Argos by her side as king, but he declines. Later Zeus appears before Perseus and offers to bring him to Olympus which he also refuses. Zeus, while disgruntled, accepts Perseus' choice and revives Io allowing them to be together.

### Alternative ending

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Alternative ending")]

After defeating the Kraken and Hades, Andromeda falls into the sea and Perseus dives in to save her. When he reaches her, he embraces her with a passionate kiss, indicating that they have fallen in love. After reaching the shore and regaining consciousness, Perseus tells Andromeda that he will return. Getting on Pegasus, he flies to Mount Olympus and confronts Zeus, stating that he does not wish to be one of them. After slamming his sword into the gods' map and shattering the models of every living person, he flies on Pegasus over the sea, presumably back to Argos.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Teide_2011.jpg/250px-Teide_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teide_2011.jpg)

[Teide National Park](/wiki/Teide_National_Park "Teide National Park") ([Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife")) is the most visited national park in Europe[[7]](#cite_note-7) and one of the most visited in the world, and place of filming of some scenes from the movie.

The *[Clash of the Titans](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(1981_film) "Clash of the Titans (1981 film)")* remake project started in 2002 under producer [Adam Schroeder](/wiki/Adam_Schroeder "Adam Schroeder") and writers [John Glenn](/wiki/John_Glenn_(screenwriter) "John Glenn (screenwriter)") and Travis Wright. They wanted to drop the "cheesy chessboard manipulation of characters" by the gods.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Wright and Glenn described their story as "an adventure film with religious elements".[[9]](#cite_note-9) Producer Basil Iwanyk revived the project in 2006 with a rewrite by [Travis Beacham](/wiki/Travis_Beacham "Travis Beacham"), a fan of the original, who intended the script to be "darker and more realistic".[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Lawrence Kasdan](/wiki/Lawrence_Kasdan "Lawrence Kasdan") and director [Stephen Norrington](/wiki/Stephen_Norrington "Stephen Norrington") signed on in 2007. Kasdan gave the script another rewrite from the Beacham version.[[11]](#cite_note-11) But Norrington was unsure about his direction for the project because he did not grow up with the original. Leterrier, who did, contacted Norrington through their shared agent about replacing him.[[12]](#cite_note-ecran-12) By June 2008 Leterrier joined the project and Warner Bros. greenlit the film.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Leterrier noted the original *Clash of the Titans* inspired the climax of his previous film *[The Incredible Hulk](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(film) "The Incredible Hulk (film)")* – a battle in a burnt-down courtroom with temple-like columns – and has compared modern superheroes to [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Writers Phil Hay and Matt Manfredi took over the script during July 2008 and used Beacham's draft as a starting point. They focused on the mythology and telling the story through Leterrier's eyes. Hay and Manfredi had to rewrite the script in less than a year using a very active process.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Leterrier sought [Ray Harryhausen](/wiki/Ray_Harryhausen "Ray Harryhausen")'s involvement.[[12]](#cite_note-ecran-12) He also reunited with *Hulk* concept artist Aaron Sims, who had already been working on *Clash of the Titans* with Norrington.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Louis Leterrier, during an interview, revealed that he is a big fan of [Masami Kurumada](/wiki/Masami_Kurumada "Masami Kurumada")'s *[Saint Seiya](/wiki/Saint_Seiya "Saint Seiya")* [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga") (also known as [Knights of the Zodiac](/wiki/Knights_of_the_Zodiac "Knights of the Zodiac")) and its anime adaptation. He specifically cited the armor that the Gods wear in his film remake as a sign of homage and respect to *Saint Seiya*. Masami Kurumada, the author of *Saint Seiya*, was even asked to collaborate with the production team on poster designs.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Sam Worthington did not wear sandals while filming; he instead painted toes on his sports shoes so he could perform the stunts better.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

According to actress [Emma Thompson](/wiki/Emma_Thompson "Emma Thompson"), she almost made an accidental uncredited cameo in the film while visiting her friend [Liam Neeson](/wiki/Liam_Neeson "Liam Neeson") on the set. Thompson, who had been filming *[Nanny McPhee and the Big Bang](/wiki/Nanny_McPhee_and_the_Big_Bang "Nanny McPhee and the Big Bang")* in an adjacent studio, went to visit Neeson during a break, just as Neeson was about to shoot a scene with [Ralph Fiennes](/wiki/Ralph_Fiennes "Ralph Fiennes") and [Danny Huston](/wiki/Danny_Huston "Danny Huston"). Unable to exit the set fast enough as the cameras began to roll, Thompson, in her clumsy [Nanny McPhee](/wiki/Nanny_McPhee "Nanny McPhee") costume, had to hide behind Huston's throne during the take so she would not be picked up by the cameras.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

For the 2D to 3D conversion, Leterrier approached the studio early on about a 3D conversion, but it was expensive and very new technology.[[21]](#cite_note-colliderint-21) After *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*, the studio put pressure on Leterrier to convert the film. He was worried because of his previous concerns but was convinced after seeing the View-D conversion process.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Leterrier considered the 3D conversion to improve the viewing experience, and states that it should not be seen as a gimmick.[[21]](#cite_note-colliderint-21) In 2013, however, Leterrier called the 3D conversion "famously rushed and famously horrible" and "a gimmick to steal money from the audience".[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Filming locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Filming locations")]

Filming began April 27, 2009, near London, at [Shepperton Studios](/wiki/Shepperton_Studios "Shepperton Studios"), and also at [Pinewood Studios](/wiki/Pinewood_Studios "Pinewood Studios") and at [Longcross Studios](/wiki/Longcross_Studios "Longcross Studios"), near Chertsey, in Surrey.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Filming also took place in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands") (Spain) (primarily at the [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), [Teide National Park](/wiki/Teide_National_Park "Teide National Park") in [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife")), [Maspalomas](/wiki/Maspalomas "Maspalomas") Dunes, [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria"), and [Timanfaya National Park](/wiki/Timanfaya_National_Park "Timanfaya National Park") in [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote"). Aerial photography was conducted in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") and [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia").[[25]](#cite_note-BW_2009-04-25)

Filming of volcano scenes at the Harriet hole in [Dinorwic Slate Quarry](/wiki/Dinorwic_Quarry "Dinorwic Quarry") in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") wrapped at the end of July.[[26]](#cite_note-26) This slate quarry has also been used for locations for *[Willow](/wiki/Willow_(1988_film) "Willow (1988 film)")* and *[Street Fighter](/wiki/Street_Fighter_(1994_film) "Street Fighter (1994 film)")*.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Release")]

*Clash of the Titans* was originally set for standard release on March 26, 2010.[[3]](#cite_note-BD_2007-10-3)[[4]](#cite_note-DC_2009-10-4) The Heat Vision Blog reported on January 27, 2010, that after a 3D conversion test of the film, which Warner Bros. found to be a "roaring success", the film would be converted to 3D and would premiere on April 2, 2010. The national premiere in Spain took place on March 30 in [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), the capital city of the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands").[[5]](#cite_note-3D-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Poster-6)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

[Review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") reports that 27% of critics have given the film a positive review based on 266 reviews; the average rating is 4.3/10. The website's critical consensus states, "An obviously affectionate remake of the 1981 original, Louis Leterrier's *Clash of the Titans* doesn't offer enough visual thrills to offset the deficiencies of its script."[[29]](#cite_note-29) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film was assigned a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_average "Weighted average") score of 39 out of 100, based on 37 reviews from mainstream critics, indicating "generally unfavorable" reviews.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "B" on an A+ to F scale.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Even before release, the film attracted some negative attention for its original tagline, "Titans Will Clash", although the trailers, edited to match "[The Bird and the Worm](/wiki/The_Bird_and_the_Worm "The Bird and the Worm")" by [The Used](/wiki/The_Used "The Used"), were praised.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Most criticism was directed at the 3D conversion, the screenplay, [Louis Leterrier](/wiki/Louis_Leterrier "Louis Leterrier")'s direction, and [Sam Worthington](/wiki/Sam_Worthington "Sam Worthington")'s performance, while the effects and supporting cast were praised.

In his review for the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")*, [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") gave the film three stars out of four, stating "I don't say it's good cinema, although I recognize the craftsmanship that went into it. I don't say it's good acting, when the men have so much facial hair they all look like [Liam Neeson](/wiki/Liam_Neeson "Liam Neeson"). I like the energy, the imagination, the silliness."[[33]](#cite_note-33) Richard Corliss of *Time* could understand why the film received negative reviews, but found it "a full-throttle action-adventure, played unapologetically straight." He dismissed other critics' complaints, writing that the film is "very watchable in 2-D," that other critics were influenced by nostalgia for the original, and that 15 seconds of Bubo is enough for his tastes.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Colin Covert gave the film a mildly positive review, stating the film was "all flash, trash, and crash," "a tasty hunk of baloney," and "mindless yet shamelessly thrilling." He said Worthington had a "[Shatneresque](/wiki/William_Shatner "William Shatner") heaviness about him," and found that all the laughs came from the fact that the heavyweight actors were "slumming through their roles."[[35]](#cite_note-35) [Owen Gleiberman](/wiki/Owen_Gleiberman "Owen Gleiberman") of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* gave the film a B−, writing "The new *Clash* isn't a cynical rehash. It has the flavor of a certain pre-CGI innocence."[[36]](#cite_note-36) [James Berardinelli](/wiki/James_Berardinelli "James Berardinelli") gave it a mixed review, concluding that "*Clash of the Titans* is a flawed but mildly entertaining regurgitation of Greek mythological elements, but it's also an example of how poorly executed 3D can hamstring a would-be spectacle."[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Peter Travers](/wiki/Peter_Travers "Peter Travers") of *Rolling Stone* awarded the film one star out of four, stating "The film is a sham, with good actors going for the paycheck and using beards and heavy makeup to hide their shame."[[38]](#cite_note-38) In a review for the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")*, Turan complained that the film is worse in 3D; he went on further to explain that the action scenes are "more of a distraction than an enhancement", with the battle scenes being cluttered and "harder to follow rather than exciting".[[39]](#cite_note-39) Claudia Puig for *USA Today* wrote that the film's "most outstanding achievement is the ability to be both chaotic and dull". Justification for her opinion came from the frantic action sequences and muddled special effects.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Dan Kois blamed the director for making a "muddled disappointment" instead of a "camp classic that could have endured for generations." He also accused Leterrier of not knowing how to direct an action scene, and that the film lacked "wit and flair."[[41]](#cite_note-41) David Stratton also criticized the film's action scenes, suggesting to Leterrier: "check out your local video store for something by [Kurosawa](/wiki/Akira_Kurosawa "Akira Kurosawa"), or almost any movie with sword fight scenes, to see how it's done."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Accolades")]

At the [2010 Teen Choice Awards](/wiki/2010_Teen_Choice_Awards "2010 Teen Choice Awards"), *Clash of the Titans* received four nominations (including [Choice Movie – Fantasy](/wiki/Teen_Choice_Award_for_Choice_Movie_%E2%80%93_Sci-Fi/Fantasy "Teen Choice Award for Choice Movie – Sci-Fi/Fantasy")).[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) It was nominated for the [Annie Award for Outstanding Achievement for Character Animation in a Live Action Production](/wiki/Annie_Award_for_Outstanding_Achievement_for_Character_Animation_in_a_Live_Action_Production "Annie Award for Outstanding Achievement for Character Animation in a Live Action Production") and the [Alliance of Women Film Journalists](/wiki/Alliance_of_Women_Film_Journalists "Alliance of Women Film Journalists")' Time Waster Remake or Sequel Award.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The film received nominations for [Worst Prequel, Remake, Rip-off or Sequel](/wiki/Golden_Raspberry_Award_for_Worst_Prequel,_Remake,_Rip-off_or_Sequel "Golden Raspberry Award for Worst Prequel, Remake, Rip-off or Sequel") and Worst Eye-Gouging Misuse of 3D at the [31st Golden Raspberry Awards](/wiki/31st_Golden_Raspberry_Awards "31st Golden Raspberry Awards").[[49]](#cite_note-49) At the [37th Saturn Awards](/wiki/37th_Saturn_Awards "37th Saturn Awards"), the film received a nomination for [Best Fantasy Film](/wiki/Saturn_Award_for_Best_Fantasy_Film "Saturn Award for Best Fantasy Film").[[50]](#cite_note-50) It placed one of the Top Box Office Films at the 2011 [ASCAP Awards](/wiki/ASCAP_Awards "ASCAP Awards").[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Clash of the Titans* earned $61,235,105 in its opening weekend in 3,777 theaters in the United States and Canada (not including Thursday previews).[[1]](#cite_note-mojo-1) The movie was #1 for two weeks in a row, edging out *[Date Night](/wiki/Date_Night "Date Night")* and the previous winner, *[How to Train Your Dragon](/wiki/How_to_Train_Your_Dragon_(2010_film) "How to Train Your Dragon (2010 film)")*.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The film held the record for having the highest April opening weekend for a Warner Bros. film until 2025 when *[A Minecraft Movie](/wiki/A_Minecraft_Movie "A Minecraft Movie")* surpassed it.[[53]](#cite_note-53) *Clash of the Titans* made $163,214,888 domestically, as of July 22, 2010, and $330,000,000 overseas, as of September 19, 2010, for a worldwide total of $493,214,888. On the [all-time worldwide chart](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films#Highest-grossing_films "List of highest-grossing films") it ranks 80th and in North America it is below #100.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Home media")]

*Clash of the Titans* was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") combo pack on July 16 (Mexico), July 26 (UK), July 27 (USA) and (Canada), October 6 (Japan), 2010. A 3D Blu-ray version of the film was also released in a combo pack with the 2D version, DVD, and digital copy.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Video game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Video game")]

Main article: [Clash of the Titans (video game)](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(video_game) "Clash of the Titans (video game)")

Namco Bandai Games and [Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Interactive_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment") released a video game adaptation of the movie on July 27, 2010, on [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") and [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360"), to coincide with the film's home video release; it was originally planned for release in March 2010.

## Sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sequel")]

Main article: [Wrath of the Titans](/wiki/Wrath_of_the_Titans "Wrath of the Titans")

Production of the sequel, *Wrath of the Titans*, directed by [Jonathan Liebesman](/wiki/Jonathan_Liebesman "Jonathan Liebesman"), began on March 23, 2011. Sam Worthington, Ralph Fiennes and Liam Neeson reprised their roles and the film was released on March 30, 2012.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In November 2011, Warner Bros. hired [Dan Mazeau](/wiki/Dan_Mazeau "Dan Mazeau") and [David Leslie Johnson](/wiki/David_Leslie_Johnson "David Leslie Johnson"), who wrote *Wrath of the Titans*, to write and develop a sequel, *[Revenge of the Titans](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(franchise) "Clash of the Titans (franchise)")*, but it was cancelled.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## Internet meme

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Internet meme")]

The phrase "Release the Kraken!", said by Liam Neeson's character Zeus in film trailers, became an Internet meme.[[58]](#cite_note-58) *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* listed it as one of the top 10 buzzwords of the year.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Among supporters of [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") the phrase has come to refer to the unfounded [voter fraud](/wiki/Voter_fraud "Voter fraud") in the [2020 US Presidential Election](/wiki/2020_US_Presidential_Election "2020 US Presidential Election").[[60]](#cite_note-60)
